vulnerability.title: Ensure auditd Collects Information on Kernel Module Unloading - delete_module
vulnerability.description: To capture kernel module loading and unloading events, use the following line, setting ARCH to either b32 for 32-bit system, or having two lines for both b32 and b64 in case your system is 64-bit: -a always,exit -F arch=ARCH -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=modules Place to add the line depends on a way auditd daemon is configured. If it is configured to use the augenrules program (the default), add the line to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d. If the auditd daemon is configured to use the auditctl utility, add the line to file /etc/audit/audit.rules.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # First perform the remediation of the syscall rule # Retrieve hardware architecture of the underlying system # Note: 32-bit and 64-bit kernel syscall numbers not always line up => # it's required on a 64-bit system to check also for the presence # of 32-bit's equivalent of the corresponding rule. # (See `man 7 audit.rules` for details ) [ "$(getconf LONG_BIT)" = "32" ] && RULE_ARCHS=("b32") || RULE_ARCHS=("b32" "b64") for ARCH in "${RULE_ARCHS[@]}" do ACTION_ARCH_FILTERS="-a always,exit -F arch=$ARCH" OTHER_FILTERS="" AUID_FILTERS="-F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset" SYSCALL="delete_module" KEY="modules" SYSCALL_GROUPING="create_module delete_module finit_module init_module query_mo

triage_decision.reason: Adding an audit rule for delete_module is a standard configuration change that does not affect system functionality or security posture beyond logging. It follows STIG hardening patterns for audit rules and is unlikely to cause lockout or breakage.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Potential for missing events if rule syntax is incorrect

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to remedy the audit rule for delete_module. The plan: We'll add a rule to /etc/audit/rules.d/module_unloading.rules or similar. Determine if auditd configured to use augenrules or auditctl. Most likely uses augenrules. We'll add file /etc/audit/rules.d/30-module-delete.rules with content: -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=modules (and similar for b64). But rule may need both arch lines.

Plan: Use read_file to check if /etc/audit/rules.d/. We'll just add a new file. Check if existing file contains this rule. If not, write. We'll ensure not duplicate. We'll run needed commands: read_file, write_file.

Also might need to restart auditd after modifying. But not in instructions; no mention to restart. But rule will be applied on next reload or restart. Maybe it's required; but if system doing immediate rule reload? In general, auditd reads at startup and reload via service auditd reload. But not mandatory.

We need to use only run_cmd, read_file, write_file. We'll create file if not exists. We'll use read_file path="/etc/audit/audit.rules" if augenrules. But easier: check presence of /etc/audit/rules.d. Typically yes. We'll attempt to write to /etc/audit/rules.d/delete_module.rules.

File content: two lines, one for b32 and one for b64.

We must check architecture using getconf LONG_BIT. Use run_cmd 'getconf LONG_BIT' to determine. But reading file content requires run_cmd, but we can run 'getconf LONG_BIT' via run_cmd. That's ok.

Plan commands:

1. run_cmd: getconf LONG_BIT to determine arch. We'll parse output. But plan may be to compute architecture programmatically in script. We can use bash to generate lines. But we can just add both lines unconditionally; it's fine for either 32 or 64. In 32-bit, b64 line may be ignored? It may not harm. The rule may trigger for wrong arch but fine.

Simpler: add both lines unconditionally.

Thus just write file with two lines. We should read_file existing content to avoid duplicate. If file exists and contains rule, skip. But file likely not exists. We'll still read to confirm.

Thus sequence:

1. run_cmd: 'getconf LONG_BIT' to get architecture variable? Actually not needed.

2. read_file: /etc/audit/rules.d/10-delete_module.rules? We'll choose file: /etc/audit/rules.d/70-delete_module.rules (to be loaded after others). But any suffix.

We'll read file /etc/audit/rules.d/delete_module.rules. If missing, we create with content.

But we may use "read_file" of the file; if missing, review_plan says? If missing, read_file returns? We'll assume it returns error; but we can proceed.

We'll just write_file to create file with content. If file exists, we may read to avoid duplicate lines. So plan: read existing content; if none or missing, skip; else check for rule string.

But to be safe, add both lines. It's fine.

Then we may need to restart auditd: run_cmd 'systemctl restart auditd'.

Thus plan: 1) read_file, 2) write_file, 3) run_cmd restart auditd.

We must use review_plan first. Provide full plan description.

review_feedback: The proposed plan adds a file with two architecture entries but omits the full rule syntax required to effectively audit the delete_module syscall with appropriate filters (auid, key, syscall). As written, the rules will not correctly capture the event and thus will not resolve the vulnerability.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed plan adds a file with two architecture entries but omits the full rule syntax required to effectively audit the delete_module syscall with appropriate filters (auid, key, syscall). As written, the rules will not correctly capture the event and thus will not resolve the vulnerability.
concerns: Missing complete rule syntax (always,exit, -S delete_module, auid filters, key).
No verification that the file is parsed correctly by auditd.
suggested_improvements: Add full audit rule lines, e.g., `-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=modules` and similarly for b64.
Check file ownership/permissions (root:root, 0640) so auditd loads it.
Consider reloading auditd with `auditctl -r /etc/audit/rules.d/10-delete_module.rules` or `systemctl restart auditd` only if file changed.
